questions.text: Walk me through the intelligence lifecycle and describe how you apply each phase operationally in a security organization.

junior: knows there are phases to intelligence work
can name some phases like collection and analysis
understands intelligence is more than just IOCs
mid: names all six phases: Planning & Direction, Collection, Processing, Analysis, Dissemination, Feedback
Planning: defines Priority Intelligence Requirements (PIRs) aligned to organizational risk and business decisions
Collection: identifies sources (commercial feeds, OSINT, ISACs, HUMINT, technical sensors) mapped to each PIR
Processing: normalization, deduplication, enrichment before analysis - raw data is not intelligence
Analysis: adding context, confidence levels, and adversary intent - the analyst's core value-add
Dissemination: tailoring output format and detail level to the audience (technical IOCs for SOC, strategic summary for CISO)
senior: distinguishes intelligence from information: information is raw data, intelligence is processed, analyzed, and contextualized for decision-making
describes feedback loop as the most neglected phase: without consumer feedback, collection and analysis drift from actual organizational needs
identifies common lifecycle breakdowns: PIRs too vague to drive collection, analysis not consumed due to poor dissemination format, no feedback loop so program doesn't improve
maps each phase to specific tools: MISP for collection/processing, Maltego for analysis, TLP-tagged reports for dissemination, quarterly PIR reviews for feedback
connects intelligence lifecycle to the IR process: threat intel feeds must translate to detection rules, hunting hypotheses, and IR playbook updates to be operationally valuable
staff: designs the intelligence program around business decision points: what decisions does leadership need to make where intelligence changes the outcome
measures program effectiveness: PIR coverage rate, intelligence-to-action conversion rate (what percentage of reports drive a concrete security control change)
addresses the 'so what' problem: intelligence that doesn't lead to a defensive action or decision has zero value regardless of analytical quality
builds the feedback mechanism formally into the program: quarterly PIR review with stakeholders, intelligence consumer satisfaction surveys, action item tracking from disseminated reports

follow_ups.probe: Give me an example of a well-formed Priority Intelligence Requirement versus a poorly formed one, and explain what makes the difference.
You've produced a high-quality analysis on a ransomware group targeting your sector. Describe specifically how the report content and format differ for the SOC analyst versus the CISO audience.
Your CTI team produces 15 reports per month. How do you know whether those reports are actually improving the organization's security posture?

follow_ups.escalate: Your CTI program has been running for 2 years. The SOC uses your IOC feeds, but strategic reports go unread by leadership and no feedback ever comes back. How do you diagnose and fix the program?

follow_ups.simplify: What is the difference between raw threat data (like an IP address from a feed) and actual threat intelligence, and why does that distinction matter?

questions.interviewer_notes: The intelligence lifecycle question calibrates whether a candidate understands CTI as a program vs. a feed subscription service. Key differentiators: knowing PIRs as the foundation that determines collection (not the reverse), understanding the feedback loop is usually broken and why it matters, and connecting intelligence output to concrete security decisions. Candidates who describe CTI as 'consuming feeds and sharing IOCs' have not operated a mature program.

questions.text: Explain the Diamond Model of Intrusion Analysis. How do you use it practically when analyzing an intrusion, and what does it add beyond ATT&CK mapping?

junior: knows the Diamond Model has four parts
can name adversary, capability, infrastructure, victim
understands it's used to analyze intrusions
mid: defines the four core features: Adversary (who is attacking), Capability (what tools/techniques are used), Infrastructure (IPs, domains, C2 servers used), Victim (the targeted organization/system)
knows the six meta-features: Timestamp, Phase, Result (success/failure), Direction (victim-to-adversary or adversary-to-victim), Methodology, Resources
uses the model to pivot analysis: if you know the capability, what other victims may have been targeted by the same adversary using the same tool?
distinguishes the Diamond Model from ATT&CK: Diamond Model analyzes a specific intrusion event and relationships between components; ATT&CK catalogs adversary behaviors across many intrusions
senior: uses the Activity Thread: connecting multiple diamond events across time to build a campaign picture (same adversary + infrastructure = linked events even if capability differs)
uses the Activity Group: clustering multiple Activity Threads to identify a threat actor's broader operational pattern across victims and time
applies the Diamond Model to attribution: consistency in the Adversary vertex across different Capability and Infrastructure suggests the same actor even if TTPs evolve

identifies analytical pivots: known Infrastructure (C2 domain) leads to new Capability (malware family) through passive DNS; known Capability (malware) leads to new Adversary infrastructure through C2 protocol analysis
distinguishes personae (the operational identity the adversary presents) from the true adversary identity - key for attribution accuracy
staff: designs Diamond Model-based threat intelligence reports that communicate all four vertices and their relationships rather than just IOC lists
uses the model to define intelligence gaps: if you know Capability but not Adversary, what collection activities fill that gap

applies Diamond Model analysis to strategic briefings: 'here is the adversary targeting us, here are their capabilities, here is the infrastructure we've observed, here is what this means for our specific risk profile'
integrates Diamond Model with ATT&CK: Diamond Model provides the campaign structure, ATT&CK fills in the behavioral detail within each Capability vertex

follow_ups.probe: You have a malware sample (Capability vertex) with a hardcoded C2 domain (Infrastructure vertex). Walk me through 3 specific pivoting actions you'd take from each vertex to expand the picture.
You have 5 intrusion events with different C2 infrastructure but the same custom malware implant. How does the Activity Thread concept help you determine if these are the same threat actor?
You've mapped the Capability and Infrastructure vertices of an intrusion. What data sources and analytical techniques would you use to populate the Adversary vertex?

follow_ups.escalate: Two intrusions in your network share the same YARA-matching malware family (capability) but use completely different infrastructure and target different business units. Using the Diamond Model, what's your analytical conclusion, and what additional collection would you need to confirm?

follow_ups.simplify: Take a real intrusion: an attacker with a Cobalt Strike beacon connecting to a domain they registered last week targeting your finance team. Map each of the four Diamond Model vertices to this scenario.

questions.interviewer_notes: Diamond Model questions reveal whether candidates have used it as an analytical framework vs. just knowing the definition. Key differentiators: understanding Activity Threads for campaign analysis (linking events), knowing how to pivot between vertices for intelligence expansion, and articulating what it adds over ATT&CK (relationship analysis vs. behavior cataloging). Candidates who only describe the four vertices without demonstrating analytical application have memorized the definition, not the methodology.

questions.text: How do you use the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain to analyze a completed intrusion retroactively and to inform a defensive strategy?

junior: can name some Kill Chain phases
knows the Kill Chain describes attacker steps
understands defenders try to interrupt it
mid: names all 7 phases: Reconnaissance, Weaponization, Delivery, Exploitation, Installation, Command & Control, Actions on Objectives
retroactive analysis: maps discovered artifacts to Kill Chain phases to reconstruct the intrusion narrative
identifies the defender's leverage point: earlier intervention = less impact (stopping at Delivery prevents exploitation; stopping at C2 prevents Actions on Objectives)
compares Kill Chain to ATT&CK: Kill Chain is linear/high-level (good for briefings), ATT&CK is granular/multi-dimensional (good for detection engineering)
defensive strategy: 'courses of action matrix' - for each phase, what can you Detect, Deny, Disrupt, Degrade, Deceive, or Destroy
senior: identifies Kill Chain limitations: assumes linear progression (attackers often skip phases or repeat them); doesn't model insider threats or supply chain attacks well; focuses on perimeter breach model which is less relevant post-cloud

applies the CKC courses of action matrix: for Delivery (spearphishing), Detect via email gateway logging, Deny via SPF/DKIM/DMARC, Disrupt via sandbox detonation, Deceive via honey credentials in the phishing simulation
uses the Kill Chain to identify detection gaps: map existing detection rules against each phase - phases with no detections are blind spots
compares Kill Chain with MITRE ATT&CK for ICS and knows when to use each based on target environment
identifies where defenders have the highest ROI: Weaponization phase is invisible to defenders; Reconnaissance is often too early; C2 detection provides high-confidence malicious verdict
staff: uses Kill Chain analysis to drive security investment decisions: 'our current controls stop attackers at Installation but not before; investing in Delivery-phase controls (email sandbox, DMARC) would push the disruption point earlier'
designs red team exercises mapped to Kill Chain phases to test coverage across all phases
briefs leadership using Kill Chain as the narrative framework: 'the attacker completed 5 of 7 phases before detection; here's what each phase meant for our risk'
combines Kill Chain (campaign structure) + Diamond Model (intrusion relationships) + ATT&CK (behavioral detail) into a complete analytical framework

follow_ups.probe: For the Exploitation phase specifically, name two specific Detect-level controls and two specific Deny-level controls your organization could implement.
You've mapped your detection rules to Kill Chain phases and find you have zero coverage at Reconnaissance. Is this a problem worth fixing? What would Reconnaissance-phase detection look like?
The Kill Chain was designed in 2011. What are its limitations when applied to modern attacks like cloud compromises, supply chain attacks, or insider threats?

follow_ups.escalate: A ransomware group targeting your sector completes Reconnaissance through Weaponization externally before any interaction with your environment. Your first possible detection is at Delivery. How do you design a defense strategy knowing you will never detect the first two phases?

follow_ups.simplify: In the Kill Chain framework, which phase offers the highest value for a defender to invest in detection and prevention, and why?

questions.interviewer_notes: Kill Chain questions reveal whether candidates use it as an analytical tool vs. just a memorized list. Key differentiators: knowing the courses of action matrix (Detect/Deny/Disrupt/Degrade/Deceive/Destroy), ability to identify Kill Chain limitations (linear model doesn't fit modern cloud/insider threats), and using it as a gap analysis tool for detection coverage. Strong candidates combine Kill Chain + Diamond Model + ATT&CK as complementary frameworks.

questions.text: Explain STIX 2.1 and TAXII 2.1. How have you used them in a production CTI program, and what are their practical limitations?

junior: knows STIX is a format for threat intelligence
knows TAXII is a transport protocol
understands they're used to share threat intelligence
mid: STIX 2.1 defines two object types: STIX Domain Objects (SDOs: Indicator, Malware, Threat Actor, Attack Pattern, Campaign, Course of Action, Intrusion Set, Tool, Vulnerability, etc.) and STIX Relationship Objects (SROs: Relationship and Sighting)
STIX uses JSON format with UUIDs, confidence scores (0-100), TLP markings via object_marking_refs, and valid_from/valid_until timestamps
TAXII 2.1 defines: Server, API Root, Collection (a logical grouping of STIX objects), and Channel objects for pub/sub - REST-based API over HTTPS
practical usage: MISP exports to STIX 2.1 for sharing with partner organizations or feeding into TIP platforms (ThreatConnect, Anomali ThreatStream, OpenCTI)
key limitation: STIX's expressive power often exceeds what SIEMs/EDR platforms can consume natively - STIX objects must usually be reduced to atomic IOCs for operational use
senior: knows STIX 2.1 improvements over 2.0: granular marking definitions, extensions, new object types (Infrastructure, Malware Analysis, Note, Language Content), and the Indicator pattern language using STIX Patterning
designs STIX patterns for behavioral indicators beyond simple IOC matching: [network-traffic:dst_port = 4444 AND process:name = 'powershell.exe'] as a STIX Indicator pattern
knows TAXII 2.1 collection types: STIX Bundles for bulk transfer, Envelope for incremental updates, supports filter parameters (added_after, match[type], match[id])
identifies STIX relationship modeling: Indicator (STIX object) 'indicates' Malware (STIX object) 'uses' Attack Pattern (STIX object) - full provenance chain from IOC to behavior to technique

addresses the trust problem in STIX sharing: STIX has no built-in vetting mechanism - a TAXII feed can publish low-confidence or false IOCs with the same STIX confidence=100 that a high-quality feed publishes
staff: designs enterprise STIX/TAXII architecture: MISP as collection and sharing hub, OpenCTI for analysis and STIX-native graph analysis, TAXII client in SIEM for automated IOC ingestion
addresses STIX operationalization gap: most SIEMs consume atomic indicators (IP, hash, domain) but not complex STIX relationship objects - requires translation layer
evaluates commercial TIP platforms (Recorded Future, Anomali, ThreatConnect, Intel 471) against open-source (MISP, OpenCTI) for organizational context
participates in trust group design: TAXII sharing communities (ISACs, FIRST, sector groups) require governance, membership vetting, and TLP enforcement policies

follow_ups.probe: You've configured MISP to export a threat actor campaign as STIX 2.1. Walk me through which STIX objects you'd expect to see in the bundle and how they relate to each other.

A TAXII feed is publishing STIX Indicator objects with confidence=100 for IP addresses. How do you evaluate whether to trust this feed's confidence scores, and what's the risk of ingesting them at face value?
Your SOC asks you to push all your STIX 2.1 intelligence into the SIEM for automated blocking. What practical challenges do you encounter, and how do you solve them?

follow_ups.escalate: You want to share a complex STIX 2.1 bundle describing a campaign with Threat Actor → uses → Malware → uses → Attack Pattern relationships, with TLP:AMBER markings and confidence scores, via TAXII to 12 partner organizations with different TIP platforms. What are the interoperability challenges, and how do you ensure the relationships survive the round-trip?

follow_ups.simplify: In plain terms, if STIX is the language, what is TAXII, and what specific problem does each solve in threat intelligence sharing?

questions.interviewer_notes: STIX/TAXII questions separate practitioners who've actually built CTI sharing programs from those who know the terms. Key differentiators: knowing STIX Relationship Objects (SROs) and the provenance chain they enable, understanding TAXII collection architecture, and recognizing the operationalization gap (STIX richness vs. SIEM atomic indicator consumption). The STIX pattern language knowledge is a strong senior differentiator.

questions.text: How do you build a threat actor profile? What data sources do you use, and how do you decide when you have enough confidence to attribute activity to a specific group?

junior: would look at threat actor reports from vendors
knows actors have names like APT28 or Lazarus
understands actors have preferred targets
mid: collects from multiple source types: technical (malware samples, infrastructure, TTPs from incident data), open-source (vendor reports, academic research, government advisories), closed-source (commercial threat intel subscriptions)

builds the actor profile around structured fields: motivations (espionage/financial/hacktivism), targets (sectors, geographies, organizational size), capabilities (sophistication level, custom vs. commodity tools), infrastructure patterns, operational tempo
maps observed TTPs to MITRE ATT&CK Navigator layer for the actor - creates visual coverage of their known technique set
uses ATT&CK group pages (e.g., G0007 for APT28, G0032 for Lazarus Group) as baseline profiles to build upon with own telemetry
senior: distinguishes between intelligence confidence levels: confirmed (multiple independent sources), probable, possible, speculative - and documents confidence with rationale
identifies actor infrastructure through pivoting: a single C2 domain leads to ASN, hosting provider, registration patterns, SSL certificate reuse, passive DNS history (RiskIQ/Censys/Shodan)
analyzes malware code characteristics for actor fingerprinting: unique string constants, custom encoding routines, error message language, compilation timestamps, debug path artifacts

recognizes naming convention challenges: APT28/Fancy Bear/Sofacy/STRONTIUM/Sednit/Pawn Storm are the same actor with different vendor names - maps across vendors using MITRE ATT&CK as the canonical reference

identifies false flag operations: actors who deliberately use leaked tools or imitate other actors' TTPs to complicate attribution (e.g., APT40 using commodity tools, Olympic Destroyer using multiple false flags simultaneously)
staff: designs the actor profile format that serves multiple audiences: technical profile (IOCs, YARA rules, ATT&CK mapping) for the SOC, narrative profile (motivations, targeting patterns, operational context) for the CISO
defines attribution confidence thresholds for organizational decision-making: what confidence level triggers executive notification, regulatory disclosure, or a specific defensive response
addresses the attribution paradox: high-confidence attribution requires sensitive intelligence sources; acting on that attribution may expose those sources
contributes to community threat actor naming disambiguation: participates in MITRE ATT&CK contributor program to link vendor-specific names to canonical ATT&CK group IDs

follow_ups.probe: You have ATT&CK Navigator layers for two suspected threat actors and they show 70% overlap in techniques. Does this mean they're the same actor? What additional analysis do you do?
You identified a C2 domain. Walk me through the specific pivoting steps and tools you'd use to expand that one domain into a broader infrastructure picture of the threat actor.
You're about to brief the CISO that a specific nation-state group is targeting your organization. What confidence level do you need before making that claim, and how do you communicate uncertainty?

follow_ups.escalate: Your analysis suggests you've identified a new, previously untracked threat actor. Their infrastructure, malware, and TTPs don't match any known group. How do you establish it as a new actor vs. an evolution of an existing one, and what does your initial report look like?

follow_ups.simplify: A vendor report names a threat actor as 'APT33.' What specific data points would you look for in your own environment to determine if that specific actor is targeting you?

questions.interviewer_notes: Threat actor profiling reveals whether candidates can build intelligence vs. just consume it. Key differentiators: naming normalization awareness (same actor, 10 vendor names), understanding false flag operations, distinguishing confidence levels formally, and infrastructure pivoting methodology. The 'new actor vs. evolution' escalation tests analytical judgment. Watch for candidates who confuse threat actor profiling with IOC matching.

questions.text: How do you manage indicator lifecycle in a production Threat Intelligence Platform? Specifically, how do you prevent indicator bloat and maintain confidence in your IOC feeds?

junior: knows IOCs are things like IP addresses, domains, and file hashes
would add new IOCs when they find them
knows old IOCs might not be valid anymore
mid: implements indicator expiration: IP addresses expire in 30-90 days (shared hosting, dynamic IPs), domains in 6-12 months, file hashes can be permanent but should be reviewed
applies TLP (Traffic Light Protocol) markings: TLP:RED (specific recipients only), TLP:AMBER (organization and partners), TLP:GREEN (community), TLP:CLEAR (public) - determines sharing scope
scores indicators by confidence: source reliability (A-F scale from NATO Intelligence), information credibility (1-6 scale), or custom scoring based on collection source type

differentiates indicator types by volatility: IP addresses are lowest quality/highest volatility (change frequently), domain names are medium, file hashes are high quality but easily changed by attacker recompile
implements sighting mechanism: each time an indicator is observed in the environment, update last_seen and increase confidence
senior: applies the Pyramid of Pain (David Bianco): Hash Values (trivial to change) < IP Addresses (easy) < Domain Names (simple) < Network/Host Artifacts (annoying) < Tools (challenging) < TTPs (tough) - targets high-pyramid indicators for durable detections
implements decay functions: indicator confidence degrades over time based on type (IP: half-life 30 days, domain: 90 days, hash: stable unless attacker recompiles, TTP: 6-12 months minimum)
distinguishes indicator quality by source: tactical intel feeds (high-volume, high-decay) vs. strategic actor profiles (low-volume, high-durability)
maintains relationship graph: indicator not in isolation but connected to campaign, threat actor, malware family - an expired C2 IP is still valuable as historical infrastructure for campaign linkage
manages false positive rate actively: tracks true positive vs. false positive ratio per feed source; sources with >10% FP rate are quarantined for manual review before blocking
staff: designs indicator management policy: defines retention periods, confidence scoring methodology, feed source quality tiers, and escalation process for high-confidence critical indicators
implements intelligence-driven blocking policy: defines the confidence threshold required for automatic block (e.g., confidence > 80 + source reliability A or B + seen in last 30 days)
builds feedback loop from SOC to TIP: every SOC investigation that confirms or refutes an indicator updates the confidence score in the TIP automatically
evaluates TIP platforms (MISP, OpenCTI, ThreatConnect, Anomali) on indicator lifecycle management capabilities and integration depth with SIEM/EDR/firewall for automated enforcement

follow_ups.probe: You've decided to focus on TTP-level detections instead of IOC-based blocking. A vendor feed provides 10,000 IP IOCs per day. How do you handle the feed while pivoting your program toward higher-pyramid detections?

You have a C2 IP address in your TIP that was last seen 45 days ago with confidence 90. Today the same IP appears in a new incident at another organization. How does this new sighting change your confidence and expiration calculation?
Your firewall is blocking based on TIP indicator feeds and the SOC reports 50 false positive alerts per day from legitimate business traffic being blocked. How do you diagnose and fix this?

follow_ups.escalate: You're managing a TIP with 2 million indicators from 15 different feeds. Your SIEM is ingesting all of them and generating 3,000 alerts per day, 85% of which are false positives from stale or low-quality indicators. Design a triage and remediation strategy to get to a manageable alert volume without losing true positive coverage.

follow_ups.simplify: You have a list of 500 IP addresses from a threat intel feed that are supposedly C2 infrastructure. Why might blocking all 500 immediately without any review be a bad idea?

questions.interviewer_notes: Indicator management is where CTI programs fail most commonly: indicator bloat leads to alert fatigue and SOC distrust of the entire program. The Pyramid of Pain framework is a strong mid/senior differentiator. Key senior concepts: decay functions, false positive rate tracking per feed source, and the distinction between volatile atomic indicators vs. durable TTP-level detections. The 2M indicator TIP cleanup escalation tests program design thinking.

questions.text: Explain the difference between strategic, operational, and tactical threat intelligence with concrete examples. How do you produce each type and who are the consumers?

junior: knows there are different levels of threat intelligence
understands tactical is more technical/IOC-based
knows strategic is for executives
mid: tactical: technical IOCs and detection artifacts (file hashes, IPs, domains, YARA rules, Sigma rules) consumed by SOC analysts, SIEM, and EDR platforms - short shelf life (hours to days)

operational: campaign-level analysis - who is attacking, what techniques they use, what infrastructure they use, what their targeting patterns are - consumed by security managers, IR teams, red teams - medium shelf life (weeks to months)

strategic: geopolitical and macroeconomic threat landscape analysis, industry-specific risk trends, threat actor motivations and long-term objectives - consumed by CISOs, Board, business units making investment decisions - long shelf life (months to years)

production differences: tactical comes from automated feeds + analyst enrichment; operational requires campaign analysis and cross-source correlation; strategic requires analyst expertise in geopolitical and business risk context
senior: identifies the gap most CTI programs fall into: good at tactical (feed subscriptions), weak at operational (requires correlation skill), and poor at strategic (requires different analytical training and audience relationship)

operational intelligence example: 'Lazarus Group is targeting cryptocurrency exchanges in your sector using a specific spearphishing lure with macro-enabled Excel files; here is the ATT&CK technique mapping and recommended detection rules' - bridges tactical IOCs with strategic context

strategic intelligence example: 'North Korean cyber operators are increasing activity correlated with cryptocurrency price volatility; this is likely financially motivated and targets organizations with crypto treasury holdings; risk is elevated over the next 6 months based on economic indicators'

addresses the production challenge: strategic intelligence requires analysts who can combine technical knowledge with geopolitical understanding - rare skill set; many programs outsource this to vendors (Recorded Future, Mandiant M-Trends, CrowdStrike Global Threat Report)
designs the intelligence tiering in the TIP: each intelligence object should be tagged with its level to route to appropriate consumers and set appropriate decay timelines
staff: drives strategic intelligence program by embedding CTI into business decision processes: M&A due diligence (does the target company have known breaches or APT access?), geographic expansion (what is the threat landscape in the new region?), supply chain risk (are our critical vendors targeted by ransomware groups?)
measures intelligence level balance: a CTI program producing only tactical intelligence is a reactive feed subscription service, not an intelligence program
designs the strategic intelligence report format: executive summary, key judgments with confidence ratings, implications for business decisions, recommended actions at leadership level
integrates CTI with GRC: strategic threat landscape informs the risk register, threat modeling, and security investment roadmap

follow_ups.probe: You produce excellent tactical IOC feeds. Your CISO asks for evidence that the CTI program is worth the $500K annual budget. How do you demonstrate value using only tactical intelligence metrics?
Your organization is considering acquiring a company in a sector heavily targeted by a nation-state APT. What strategic intelligence would you produce to support the M&A due diligence process?
You have tactical IOC feeds and strategic threat reports. What's the intelligence type in between, and why is it often the most useful for security teams in practice?

follow_ups.escalate: The Board asks for a 5-minute brief on the cyber threat landscape as it relates to your company's 3-year strategic plan expansion into Southeast Asia. What does your briefing cover, and how do you translate threat intelligence into business-relevant risk language?

follow_ups.simplify: Give me a concrete example of a tactical intelligence artifact, an operational intelligence report, and a strategic intelligence assessment - all three describing the same ransomware group from different angles.

questions.interviewer_notes: Intelligence levels questions reveal whether candidates have operated at all three levels vs. just tactical IOC handling. Key differentiators: recognizing the operational gap (most programs do tactical well but miss the campaign-level analysis), ability to produce strategic intelligence for non-technical executives, and connecting strategic intelligence to business decisions (M&A, geographic expansion, supply chain). Candidates who only describe tactical intelligence indicate limited program maturity exposure.

questions.text: Describe your approach to dark web monitoring. What are you looking for, how do you set it up safely, and how do you operationalize findings?

junior: knows the dark web has criminal marketplaces
knows credentials and data are sold there
understands it's risky to access directly
mid: monitors for: credential dumps (employee emails in breach databases), corporate data leaks (intellectual property, financial documents), access brokers selling VPN/RDP access to your network, ransomware group victim postings, threat actor chatter about targeting your organization
uses commercial services (Recorded Future, Digital Shadows/ReliaQuest, Flashpoint, Intel 471, Kela) rather than direct dark web access for most use cases
sets up automated alerts for brand/domain keywords, executive names, specific IP ranges, leaked credential patterns (email@company.com format)
when direct dark web access is needed: uses Tor Browser in an isolated VM with no corporate network connection, no corporate accounts, and a dedicated non-attributable persona
senior: understands the dark web ecosystem: Tor hidden services (.onion), I2P (Invisible Internet Project), Telegram channels (increasingly more criminal activity has moved here), paste sites (pastebin.com, hastebin), private criminal forums (RaidForums successor BreachForums, Genesis Market alternatives)

distinguishes access broker intelligence: initial access brokers selling corporate network access are a pre-ransomware indicator; purchase price and access type (domain admin vs. user) indicates severity level
validates dark web intelligence before acting: credential dumps may be old/not from your organization; claims of network access may be fraudulent; verify with internal IR investigation

operationalizes findings through IR playbook: confirmed credential exposure triggers password reset + session revocation + investigation for whether credentials were used; confirmed network access sale triggers immediate incident response escalation

identifies ransomware group leak sites (RaaS operators maintain dedicated leak sites for double-extortion): FIN7 (Bashe), LockBit 3.0 site, ALPHV/BlackCat, Cl0p - monitoring these identifies if your organization is being extorted
staff: designs dark web monitoring program with legal review: accessing some dark web forums may violate CFAA; purchasing stolen data to verify its authenticity has legal implications in some jurisdictions

builds intelligence sharing protocol for dark web findings: information about criminal activity affecting other organizations should be shared through appropriate channels (FBI IC3, CISA, sector ISAC) with legal guidance
addresses attribution limitations: dark web forum handles and criminal personas are not reliably linked to real identities without law enforcement assistance

integrates dark web monitoring into the broader threat intelligence lifecycle: dark web findings feed PIRs, update threat actor profiles, and trigger specific IR activities with documented response playbooks

follow_ups.probe: Dark web monitoring returns 500 corporate email addresses with associated passwords from a breach dump. How do you validate which are real, current, and represent genuine risk before triggering mass password resets?

You find a forum post offering 'domain admin access' to a company matching your organization's profile for $15,000. How do you validate this is your organization and what's your immediate response process?
Most criminal activity discussion has migrated from traditional dark web forums to encrypted messaging. What does this mean for your dark web monitoring approach and tooling?

follow_ups.escalate: Your dark web monitoring shows your organization is listed on a ransomware group's victim blog with a 72-hour countdown to data release. No internal alerts have fired. This may be your first indication of a breach. What do you do in the first hour?

follow_ups.simplify: Without accessing the dark web directly, what commercial or freely available services would you use to monitor for your organization's data or credentials appearing in criminal marketplaces?

questions.interviewer_notes: Dark web monitoring questions reveal operational security awareness and practical CTI tooling experience. Key differentiators: knowing that commercial services are standard practice for most use cases (not direct Tor access), understanding the shift to Telegram as the new dark web, and having a structured operationalization process for findings. The ransomware blog escalation is an excellent test: many organizations first learn of a breach this way. Watch for candidates who don't distinguish validated from unvalidated intelligence.

questions.text: How do you approach threat actor attribution, and what are the real limitations and risks of attribution analysis?

junior: knows attribution means identifying who did the attack
knows it's difficult to attribute
understands nation-states are common threat actors
mid: attribution uses multiple evidence types: technical (malware code, infrastructure, TTPs), operational (targeting patterns, victim sectors, timing), and strategic (geopolitical context, motivation alignment)

technical attribution: code similarity analysis (BinDiff for binary comparison, YARA cross-reference), infrastructure reuse (ASN, hosting provider, SSL certificate patterns via Shodan/RiskIQ/Censys), operational security failures (developer debug paths, language artifacts in code)
confidence levels are mandatory: 'we assess with moderate confidence that this is APT28 based on shared infrastructure with previously attributed campaigns' vs. definitively claiming attribution
distinguishes from false flag operations: sophisticated actors deliberately imitate other actors' toolsets (Olympic Destroyer used malware characteristics of Lazarus, APT3, Sofacy simultaneously)
senior: knows the four attribution models: technical (forensic evidence), behavioral (TTP consistency), operational (targeting and timing), and strategic (geopolitical and political context) - each adds independent evidence

identifies the intelligence source requirement for high-confidence attribution: technical evidence alone is insufficient for nation-state attribution; requires combination with SIGINT, HUMINT, or law enforcement that private sector analysts don't have access to

understands the attribution paradox: publicizing attribution can compromise the intelligence sources (classified) that made the attribution possible; private sector attribution is typically lower-confidence than government attribution

discusses the Diamond Model personae concept: the 'adversary' you observe may be a contractor, criminal-for-hire, or false flag - attribution to a nation-state government requires linking the observed operation to state direction, not just state-associated actors

knows common attribution pitfalls: timestamps in malware compile times reflect developer timezone (but actors know this and may falsify); keyboard layout artifacts in code (but actors know this too); language artifacts in strings (frequently seeded as false flags)
staff: designs the attribution policy: at what confidence threshold does the organization act on attribution in communications, legal filings, or regulatory reports

addresses legal implications: public attribution without strong evidence exposes the organization to defamation claims; private attribution for defensive use has different standards than public claims
works with government partners (FBI, CISA, sector-specific agencies) for attribution support: government has sources and methods unavailable to private sector; sharing intelligence flows both ways

communicates attribution with appropriate nuance to leadership: 'we believe with moderate confidence that a China-nexus group is responsible' means very different things to a lawyer preparing regulatory filings vs. a SOC analyst updating a threat actor profile

follow_ups.probe: You're analyzing malware that has Korean language artifacts, Korean developer paths, and code similarities to Lazarus Group tools - but your geopolitical assessment suggests North Korea has no motive to target this organization. How do you proceed with attribution?

Your analysis supports 'moderate confidence' nation-state attribution. The CISO wants to send a letter to customers attributing the breach to a foreign government. What concerns do you raise, and what would you need to increase confidence?

A private sector CTI firm publishes a report attributing an attack to a specific government APT group. How should you evaluate that attribution claim, and what evidence would they need to demonstrate high-confidence attribution?

follow_ups.escalate: You have a new intrusion where the TTPs match APT29, the infrastructure overlaps with APT28, and the malware code has Lazarus Group artifacts. Each of these is a well-known Russian or North Korean actor. The victim is a NATO partner organization. How do you analyze this, and what's your working hypothesis about the 'false flag' vs. 'novel actor' vs. 'tactical evolution' possibilities?

follow_ups.simplify: Name three specific technical artifacts that analysts use to link malware samples or intrusions to a known threat actor, and explain one key limitation of each.

questions.interviewer_notes: Attribution questions are the deepest test of CTI analytical maturity. Key differentiators: knowing government-private sector intelligence source asymmetry (private sector cannot achieve government-level confidence), understanding false flag operations at a sophisticated level (Olympic Destroyer is the canonical example), and having a formal confidence framework. The multi-actor attribution escalation is designed to find analysts who've grappled with the messiness of real attribution vs. clean textbook cases.

questions.text: How do you use OSINT to build intelligence on threat actors and emerging threats? What are your primary sources and methodologies?

junior: would search Google for threat actor names
knows VirusTotal is a useful resource
would look at security news sites
mid: technical OSINT: VirusTotal (malware analysis and cross-reference), Shodan (internet-facing device discovery and historical scan data), Censys (certificate and infrastructure analysis), URLScan.io (URL screenshot and content analysis), Any.run (malware sandbox)
passive DNS analysis: SecurityTrails, RiskIQ PassiveTotal, DomainTools - resolve historical DNS records to identify actor infrastructure changes and relationships between domains
network OSINT: Shodan searches for specific banner patterns (ssl.cert.subject.cn, http.html contains, port-specific searches) to identify C2 infrastructure before it appears in threat reports

social media OSINT: Twitter/X security community for real-time threat discussion, GitHub for leaked malware source code and POC exploits, paste sites for credential dumps and threat actor announcements
government/trusted sources: CISA advisories, US-CERT alerts, vendor threat reports (Mandiant/Google Threat Analysis Group, CrowdStrike, Microsoft MSTIC, Palo Alto Unit42)
senior: creates infrastructure profiles using certificate pivoting: a TLS certificate with specific subject fields (O=, CN=) appears on multiple IPs - find all IPs using the same certificate via Censys certificate search

hunts for C2 infrastructure proactively: Shodan/Censys searches for specific Cobalt Strike beacon headers, Metasploit default certificates, JARM fingerprints of known C2 frameworks before they appear in victim telemetry

JARM fingerprinting: a unique 62-character fingerprint of TLS server implementation that identifies specific C2 frameworks (Cobalt Strike, Metasploit, Sliver) across the internet without connecting to them
code analysis OSINT: searching GitHub for malware builder source code, hardcoded credentials, infrastructure artifacts leaked by threat actor operational security failures

applies structured analytic techniques: Analysis of Competing Hypotheses (ACH) to prevent cognitive bias in OSINT analysis; source triangulation (minimum 3 independent sources) before high-confidence assessments
staff: designs OSINT collection plan mapped to specific PIRs: which sources answer which intelligence questions, collection frequency, automated vs. manual collection
builds automated OSINT collection pipeline: Shodan Monitor for real-time C2 infrastructure alerts, GreyNoise for identifying scanning IPs, theHarvester for external exposure monitoring

addresses OSINT legal and operational security constraints: creating analytical personas for dark web access (not linked to organizational infrastructure), avoiding tipping off threat actors through obvious research footprints
contributes to open-source threat intelligence community: publishing sanitized IOCs to OTX, MISP, VirusTotal; participating in ISACs; contributing to MITRE ATT&CK technique additions

follow_ups.probe: You want to identify all Cobalt Strike C2 servers on the internet before they appear in threat reports. Write out a specific Shodan or Censys query you would use, and explain what each field in the query is targeting.

Passive DNS shows a suspicious domain resolved to 3 different IPs over the past 6 months before going dark. How do you use each of those IPs to expand your understanding of the threat actor's infrastructure?

You want to identify C2 infrastructure being staged by a threat actor before it's used against you. What OSINT technique allows you to fingerprint internet-facing servers by their TLS implementation characteristics, and how is this different from certificate analysis?

follow_ups.escalate: A new zero-day is being exploited in the wild. It was disclosed 6 hours ago with no patch available. Using only OSINT, how do you within 4 hours determine: which threat actors are likely using it, who in your environment is at risk, and what compensating controls exist?

follow_ups.simplify: You've found a suspicious IP address in your logs. Name four free OSINT tools you would use to investigate it, and what specific question each one helps you answer.

questions.interviewer_notes: OSINT methodology reveals hands-on CTI experience. Key differentiators: specific Shodan/Censys search syntax knowledge, JARM fingerprinting for C2 identification (increasingly important as certificate reuse becomes less common), and knowing passive DNS analysis for infrastructure pivoting. The zero-day response escalation tests time-pressured OSINT triage methodology. Watch for candidates who rely entirely on vendor reports rather than conducting original OSINT analysis.

questions.text: How do you develop and prioritize intelligence requirements (IRs) for your organization? Walk me through the process from initial stakeholder engagement through operationalizing the final requirements.

junior: knows intelligence requirements guide what the team focuses on
understands different teams have different needs
knows the CEO cares about different things than the SOC
mid: starts with stakeholder interviews: what decisions are you making where better threat information would change the outcome? (CISO, business unit leaders, SOC manager, IR team lead, risk management)
distinguishes PIRs (Priority Intelligence Requirements - board-level, organization-critical) from SIRs (Specific Intelligence Requirements - derived from PIRs, more granular collection questions)

example PIR: 'What nation-state threats currently target organizations in our sector and geography?' Derived SIR: 'Which APT groups have targeted financial services organizations in the EU in the past 12 months?'
maps IRs to collection sources: each IR should have identified collection methods (commercial feeds, OSINT, ISAC membership, government partnerships, internal telemetry)
reviews IRs quarterly at minimum with stakeholders - threat landscape changes, business priorities change, IRs must evolve
senior: identifies the common failure mode: CTI teams define IRs without stakeholder input, then produce intelligence nobody uses because it doesn't align with actual decision-making needs
applies the intelligence question decomposition: PIR → SIR → Specific Collection Actions → Specific Analytical Products - each level becomes more specific and actionable

addresses the gap between business language and intelligence language: translates 'are we at risk from ransomware?' into specific, answerable intelligence questions: 'Which ransomware groups target organizations of our size in our sector? What initial access vectors do they prefer? Are our current controls adequate against those vectors?'

designs IRs that drive measurable collection and analysis activities: a good IR has a clear answer state (what evidence would satisfy this requirement?), a named owner, a review cadence, and tied collection sources

knows the difference between standing IRs (ongoing collection requirements for persistent threats) and ad-hoc IRs (triggered by specific events like geopolitical changes, newly disclosed vulnerabilities, competitor breach)
staff: facilitates annual IR development workshop with C-suite participation: connects cyber threat intelligence requirements to business risk appetite and strategic objectives
designs the IR governance process: how new IRs are submitted, prioritized, funded, and reviewed; prevents IR sprawl (too many low-priority IRs that dilute focus)
measures IR fulfillment rate: what percentage of PIRs have current, actionable intelligence products? Gaps drive program investment decisions
connects IR process to the broader risk management framework: CTI PIRs should directly align to top-rated risks on the organizational risk register

follow_ups.probe: You're interviewing the CFO about intelligence requirements. She says 'I want to know if we're going to get hacked.' How do you translate that into a specific, actionable intelligence requirement?
Give me an example of a well-formed PIR and the 3-4 SIRs you would derive from it for a mid-size financial services organization.
You define IRs in January. In June, a new ransomware group emerges that specifically targets your sector. How does your IR process accommodate this new threat without creating IR sprawl?

follow_ups.escalate: Your organization is expanding into healthcare (previously a pure financial services company) through acquisition. How do you completely revise the CTI program's intelligence requirements to cover the new combined threat landscape without doubling the team's workload?

follow_ups.simplify: You're starting a CTI program from scratch. Before you subscribe to any threat feeds or hire analysts, what's the first thing you need to establish, and who do you talk to?

questions.interviewer_notes: Intelligence requirements development is the foundation of a mature CTI program, but it's frequently skipped in favor of immediately subscribing to feeds. Key differentiators: understanding the PIR → SIR hierarchy, knowing that IRs must come from stakeholder business decisions (not analyst preferences), and having a formal review/governance process. The CFO interview question tests ability to translate vague executive concerns into specific intelligence questions.

questions.text: Describe how you measure the maturity and business value of a CTI program. What metrics do you track and how do you present CTI value to non-technical leadership?

junior: would track number of IOCs collected
knows the program should produce threat reports
understands leadership wants to know if the program is worth the cost
mid: volume metrics (low value but easy): number of IOCs ingested, number of reports produced, number of threat actors tracked, number of source integrations
coverage metrics (medium value): ATT&CK technique coverage percentage for top threat actors, percentage of PIRs with current intelligence products, number of sectors and geographies monitored

operational impact metrics (high value): number of SOC alerts attributed to CTI-sourced indicators, number of detections deployed based on CTI findings, mean time to indicator integration (time from threat report to deployed detection)

cost avoidance framing: if CTI-sourced indicators blocked 3 phishing campaigns that statistically would have led to 0.3 ransomware incidents at $2M average cost, the attributable value is $600K against a program cost of $200K
senior: designs the 4 outcome-based CTI value metrics: Intelligence-to-Action conversion rate (what % of reports drive a specific defensive change), Threat Coverage Score (ATT&CK techniques covered by current detections vs. top actor technique set), Detection Lead Time (how many hours before a threat is widely reported does CTI enable detection), Mean Time to Contextualize (how quickly does CTI enrich a SOC alert with actor/campaign context)
applies CTI Maturity Models: FCM (Forrester Capability Maturity) levels 1-5, or CREST CTI maturity model - benchmarks against industry peers to identify investment areas
builds the retrospective CTI value case: document specific incidents where CTI-sourced intelligence directly influenced a security decision (blocked campaign, early detection, faster containment)
identifies leading vs. lagging indicators: indicator volume is a lagging indicator (just shows consumption); Detection Lead Time is a leading indicator (shows forward-looking value)
staff: presents CTI value in business risk language for the board: 'our CTI program reduced our exposure window to the top 3 threat actors targeting our sector from 45 days (industry average) to 6 days (internal measure) by enabling proactive detection deployment'

builds the CTI program business case: benchmark program cost against breach cost (average $4.45M per IBM Cost of a Data Breach 2023), probability reduction attributable to CTI investment, and cost per breach-week-prevented
designs annual CTI program review: stakeholder satisfaction survey, PIR fulfillment rate, defensive impact metrics, gap analysis against peer organizations, and next-year investment proposal

addresses the difficulty of attribution: CTI provides probabilistic risk reduction, not deterministic breach prevention; communicate in risk reduction terms rather than claiming 'CTI stopped X attacks'

follow_ups.probe: You track 500,000 IOCs ingested per month as your primary program metric. Your CISO asks whether the program is worth $400K/year. Why is IOC volume an insufficient answer, and what metric would you substitute?
Your ATT&CK Navigator layer shows 65% technique coverage for your top threat actor. Is 65% good or bad, and how do you decide where to invest to improve coverage most efficiently?
You've been running the CTI program for 2 years. A new CISO asks you to justify the program's existence in terms of business value. What data do you pull together and how do you frame the case?

follow_ups.escalate: Your CTI team produced a report 3 weeks ago warning that a ransomware group was targeting organizations in your sector using a specific vulnerability. The organization didn't patch. Now you're in a ransomware incident response. How do you document and present the CTI program's contribution to this incident in the post-incident review without being self-serving?

follow_ups.simplify: A new CISO joins and immediately asks 'how do I know if the threat intelligence program is working?' Name two metrics that actually demonstrate CTI value in operational security terms.

questions.interviewer_notes: CTI program maturity measurement separates program leaders from analysts. Key differentiators: moving beyond volume metrics to outcome-based metrics (Intelligence-to-Action rate, Detection Lead Time), using formal maturity models (FCM, CREST), and being able to present CTI value in business risk language. The ransomware incident escalation tests self-awareness and organizational communication skills. Watch for candidates who equate program value with IOC count or report volume.
